question: A student is on a walk. The student travels 1.8 km North and then 3.5 km East. [Diagram: A map showing a path starting at X, going North, then turning East to point Y.]

parts.text: By scale diagram or otherwise, determine the magnitude of the displacement from point X to point Y.
By scale diagram or otherwise, determine the direction of the displacement from point X to point Y.
The student takes 1 hour and 15 minutes to walk from point X to point Y. Calculate the average velocity of the student in $\text{km h}^{-1}$.

question: An athlete is training for a race. The graph shows how the velocity of the athlete varies with time. [Diagram: A $v-t$ graph showing a constant acceleration from 0 to $8.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ in 2.0 s, then constant velocity for 4.0 s, then constant deceleration to 0 in 3.0 s.]

parts.text: Using the graph, determine the time at which the athlete reached a velocity of $8.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.
Calculate the acceleration of the athlete during the first 2.0 s of the run.
Determine the total distance traveled by the athlete.

question: A small aircraft of mass 1400 kg is flying at a constant altitude. [Diagram: An aircraft in flight with arrows representing Lift, Weight, Thrust, and Drag.]

parts.text: The aircraft is flying at a constant speed. State the relationship between the thrust and the drag.
Calculate the weight of the aircraft.
The pilot increases the thrust to $4200\text{ N}$. The drag is $3600\text{ N}$. Calculate the acceleration of the aircraft.

question: A ball is kicked from the ground with a horizontal velocity of $15\text{ m s}^{-1}$. It lands 25 m away.

parts.text: Calculate the time the ball is in the air.
Calculate the vertical velocity of the ball just before it hits the ground.

question: A space probe is sent to orbit Venus. [Diagram: A probe in orbit around a planet.]

parts.text: The probe orbits at an altitude where the gravitational field strength is $8.1\text{ N kg}^{-1}$. Calculate the weight of the 450 kg probe at this altitude.
Using your knowledge of physics, comment on the benefits of space exploration and the risks associated with it.
parts.markingScheme: The candidate will gain credit for the breadth and/or depth of their conceptual understanding (marked out of 3).

question: The Big Bang Theory explains the origin of the universe.

parts.text: State two pieces of evidence that support the Big Bang Theory.
parts.answer: Redshift of galaxies and Cosmic Microwave Background Radiation (CMBR).

parts.text: Determine the age of the universe according to the Big Bang Theory.

question: A block of copper of mass 2.0 kg is heated from $20^\circ\text{C}$ to $80^\circ\text{C}$ using a 500 W heater.

parts.text: Calculate the heat energy required to increase the temperature of the block.
Calculate the minimum time the heater must be switched on to provide this energy.

topic: Pressure, Kinetic Theory and Gas Laws
subtopic: Pressure, Kinetic Theory and Gas Laws
question: A weather balloon contains $12\text{ m}^3$ of helium gas at a pressure of $101\text{ kPa}$ at ground level.

parts.text: As the balloon rises, the pressure of the helium decreases to $45\text{ kPa}$. Calculate the new volume of the helium, assuming the temperature remains constant.
parts.topicTag: Pressure, Kinetic Theory and Gas Laws

parts.text: Explain, using the kinetic model, why the pressure of the gas decreases as the volume increases at constant temperature.
parts.markingScheme:
• Volume increases so particles hit the walls less often (1)
• Total force on the walls decreases (1)
• $P = \frac{F}{A}$ so pressure decreases (1)
parts.topicTag: Pressure, Kinetic Theory and Gas Laws

question: A student sets up the circuit shown to investigate the resistance of a resistor. [Diagram: A circuit with a battery, an ammeter, a resistor, and a voltmeter across the resistor.]

parts.text: The reading on the ammeter is $0.40\text{ A}$ and the reading on the voltmeter is $6.0\text{ V}$. Calculate the resistance of the resistor.
The student adds another identical resistor in parallel with the first one. State what happens to the reading on the ammeter.

question: An electric kettle is rated at $2.2\text{ kW}, 230\text{ V}$.

parts.text: Calculate the current in the kettle when it is operating at its rated power.
Calculate the resistance of the heating element in the kettle.

question: A patient is treated with radiation. A mass of $0.50\text{ kg}$ of tissue receives $4.0\text{ mJ}$ of energy from alpha particles.

parts.text: Calculate the absorbed dose received by the tissue.
The radiation weighting factor for alpha particles is 20. Calculate the equivalent dose received by the tissue.

question: A sound wave travels through air at $340\text{ m s}^{-1}$. The frequency of the wave is $250\text{ Hz}$.

parts.text: Calculate the wavelength of the sound wave.
Sound waves are longitudinal. State what is meant by the term longitudinal wave.
parts.answer: The particles vibrate in the same direction as the wave travels.
parts.markingScheme: • Particles vibrate parallel to/in the same direction as the energy travel (1)

question: A ray of light enters a glass block as shown. [Diagram: A ray of light entering a glass block at an angle to the normal.]

parts.text: State what happens to the speed of the light as it enters the glass block.
Calculate the angle of refraction if the angle of incidence is $40^\circ$ and the refractive index of glass is 1.50.

question: A radioactive source is used in a hospital. The graph shows the activity of the source over time. [Diagram: An activity vs time graph showing activity decreasing from $800\text{ kBq}$ to $100\text{ kBq}$ over 180 days.]

parts.text: State what is meant by the term background count rate.
parts.answer: The radiation that is present from natural or artificial sources all the time.

parts.text: Using the graph, determine the half-life of the source.
Determine the time it takes for the corrected count rate to reduce to one eighth of its initial value.
Determine the initial corrected count rate of the source at the time it was implanted.
State one other use of nuclear radiation.
